Experimental Engineering thermal-convection loops have shown adherent wall deposits in colder regions and spongy dendritic plugs in hotter regions in three experiments using loops of $\frac{1}{2}$ -in. nickel pipe approximately 90 in. in circumference.
Velocities of about 25 ft/min and a temperature differential of 760 to $680^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ prevailed in these experiments.

Temperature gradient loops with centrifugal circulation utilizing a temperature differential of 815 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with velocities of 15 ft/sec gave massive wall deposits in cooler regions.
Corrosion tests in sealed crucibles with a temperature differential of 815 to $730^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ from top to bottom gave rise to a crystalline deposit in the colder portion.

A single low-velocity thermal convection loop loaded under hydrogen utilizing a temperature differential of 700 to $425^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ developed a scattered deposit on the cold wall with small evidence of dendritic plugging in in 300 hr of operation.
Corrosion tests in sealed tubes with temperature cycling showed small amounts of deposited metal after 12 cycles of 8 hr each from 800 to $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ using helium gas inside sealed tubes.
Isothermal corrosion tests (25 experiments) in capsules sealed under helium and heated in a vacuum at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 100 hr showed erratic weight losses but no significant mass transfer.

Temperature-gradient corrosion tests with hydrogen covering the melt and surrounding a vertical tube 17 in. long, $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at top, $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at middle, and $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at bottom, gave no
evidence of mass transfer and very little oxidation product in 100 hr.
